claim: The -p / --print flag runs Claude Code non-interactively and was previously called headless mode; all CLI options work with -p including --continue, --allowedTools, and --output-format.
quote: The CLI was previously called "headless mode." The `-p` flag and all CLI options work the same way... Add the `-p` (or `--print`) flag to any `claude` command to run it non-interactively. All CLI options work with `-p`.

claim: --output-format accepts text (default), json (structured envelope with result, session ID, metadata), or stream-json (newline-delimited JSON for real-time streaming).
quote: Use `--output-format` to control how responses are returned: `text` (default): plain text output; `json`: structured JSON with result, session ID, and metadata; `stream-json`: newline-delimited JSON for real-time streaming

claim: Stream-json mode requires --verbose and pairs with --include-partial-messages to receive token-level deltas as JSON events.
quote: Use `--output-format stream-json` with `--verbose` and `--include-partial-messages` to receive tokens as they're generated. Each line is a JSON object representing an event

claim: A typical Slack-bot pipeline captures session_id from the json result envelope and passes it back via --resume to continue the same conversation.
quote:
session_id=$(claude -p "Start a review" --output-format json | jq -r '.session_id')
claude -p "Continue that review" --resume "$session_id"

claim: system/api_retry events expose attempt, max_retries, retry_delay_ms, error_status and a categorical error string (rate_limit, server_error, etc.) with a session_id, so a Slack relay can surface backoff state.
quote: When an API request fails with a retryable error, Claude Code emits a `system/api_retry` event before retrying... `error` string error category: `authentication_failed`, `billing_error`, `rate_limit`, `invalid_request`, `server_error`, `max_output_tokens`, or `unknown`

claim: system/init is the first stream event and reports model, tools, MCP servers and loaded plugins (with plugins[] and plugin_errors[]), letting CI fail when an MCP server failed to load.
quote: The `system/init` event reports session metadata including the model, tools, MCP servers, and loaded plugins. It is the first event in the stream... Use the plugin fields to fail CI when a plugin did not load

claim: --bare skips auto-discovery of hooks, skills, plugins, MCP servers, auto-memory and CLAUDE.md for reproducible scripted runs and is the recommended mode for SDK calls; it will become the -p default.
quote: Add `--bare` to reduce startup time by skipping auto-discovery of hooks, skills, plugins, MCP servers, auto memory, and CLAUDE.md... `--bare` is the recommended mode for scripted and SDK calls, and will become the default for `-p` in a future release.

claim: In bare mode, OAuth and keychain reads are skipped so Anthropic auth must come from ANTHROPIC_API_KEY or an apiKeyHelper in --settings JSON.
quote: Bare mode skips OAuth and keychain reads. Anthropic authentication must come from `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` or an `apiKeyHelper` in the JSON passed to `--settings`.

claim: --allowedTools auto-approves tool patterns (e.g. Bash(git diff *)) without prompting; alternatively a --permission-mode like acceptEdits or dontAsk sets a session-wide baseline.
quote: Use `--allowedTools` to let Claude use certain tools without prompting... `dontAsk` denies anything not in your `permissions.allow` rules or the read-only command set, which is useful for locked-down CI runs. `acceptEdits` lets Claude write files without prompting

claim: --permission-mode accepts default, acceptEdits, plan, auto, dontAsk, or bypassPermissions and overrides defaultMode from settings files.
quote: Begin in a specified permission mode. Accepts `default`, `acceptEdits`, `plan`, `auto`, `dontAsk`, or `bypassPermissions`. Overrides `defaultMode` from settings files

claim: --permission-prompt-tool lets a non-interactive run delegate permission decisions to a named MCP tool, the documented hook for routing approvals through e.g. a Slack approval bot.
quote: Specify an MCP tool to handle permission prompts in non-interactive mode... `claude -p --permission-prompt-tool mcp_auth_tool "query"`

claim: --mcp-config loads MCP server definitions from JSON files or strings, and --strict-mcp-config restricts the run to only those servers, ignoring user/project MCP config.
quote: `--mcp-config` Load MCP servers from JSON files or strings (space-separated)... `--strict-mcp-config` Only use MCP servers from `--mcp-config`, ignoring all other MCP configurations

claim: --max-turns and --max-budget-usd are print-mode-only ceilings that cause the run to exit with an error; --no-session-persistence prevents writing the session to disk; --session-id forces a UUID.
quote: `--max-turns` Limit the number of agentic turns (print mode only). Exits with an error when the limit is reached... `--no-session-persistence` Disable session persistence so sessions are not saved to disk and cannot be resumed (print mode only)... `--session-id` Use a specific session ID for the conversation (must be a valid UUID)

claim: --fork-session combined with --resume creates a new session ID seeded with the original's history, useful for branching a Slack thread off an existing conversation.
quote: When resuming, create a new session ID instead of reusing the original (use with `--resume` or `--continue`)

claim: claude auth status exits 0 if logged in and 1 if not, and claude setup-token generates a long-lived OAuth token suitable for CI and bot processes.
quote: Show authentication status as JSON. Use `--text` for human-readable output. Exits with code 0 if logged in, 1 if not... `claude setup-token` Generate a long-lived OAuth token for CI and scripts.

claim: Sessions are persisted automatically as JSONL transcripts at ~/.claude/projects/<encoded-cwd>/<session-id>.jsonl; resuming with a mismatched cwd silently yields a fresh session.
quote: Sessions are stored under `~/.claude/projects/<encoded-cwd>/*.jsonl`, where `<encoded-cwd>` is the absolute working directory with every non-alphanumeric character replaced by `-`... If your resume call runs from a different directory, the SDK looks in the wrong place.

claim: session_id appears on every result message and, in TypeScript, on the init SystemMessage too; capture it from there to drive resume/fork.
quote: Read it from the `session_id` field on the result message (`ResultMessage` in Python, `SDKResultMessage` in TypeScript), which is present on every result regardless of success or error. In TypeScript the ID is also available earlier as a direct field on the init `SystemMessage`

claim: Continue picks up the most recent session in cwd without an ID; resume targets a specific ID (needed for multi-user apps); fork copies history into a new session for branching.
quote: Continue finds the most recent session in the current directory. You don't track anything... Resume takes a specific session ID. You track the ID. Required when you have multiple sessions (for example, one per user in a multi-user app)... Fork is different: it creates a new session that starts with a copy of the original's history.

claim: Python's ClaudeSDKClient holds session state across .query() calls inside an async-with block; the stable TypeScript query() relies on continue:true to pick up the most recent session.
quote: ClaudeSDKClient handles session IDs internally. Each call to `client.query()` automatically continues the same session... The stable TypeScript SDK... doesn't have a session-holding client object like Python's ClaudeSDKClient. Instead, pass `continue: true` on each subsequent `query()` call

claim: To resume across hosts (Slack worker pods, serverless), either ship the JSONL transcript to the same path on the new host or capture distilled state and pass it into a fresh prompt; a SessionStore adapter exists for shared storage.
quote: Move the session file. Persist `~/.claude/projects/<encoded-cwd>/<session-id>.jsonl` from the first run and restore it to the same path on the new host before calling `resume`... To resume sessions across machines or in serverless environments, mirror transcripts to shared storage with a SessionStore adapter.

claim: The Python SDK's CanUseTool callback (Callable[[str, dict, ToolPermissionContext], Awaitable[PermissionResult]]) is the headless equivalent of an interactive permission prompt and returns PermissionResultAllow with optional updated_input or PermissionResultDeny with a message.
quote: CanUseTool: Callable[[str, dict[str, Any], ToolPermissionContext], Awaitable[PermissionResult]]... PermissionResult is a union that can be either PermissionResultAllow (with optional updated_input and updated_permissions) or PermissionResultDeny (with a message and optional interrupt flag).
